titleStmt.title: Letter from Bertram Cubitt to Matthew Nathan, 23 May 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Bertram Cubitt

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: A letter from Bertram Cubitt (1862-1942) of the British War Office to Matthew Nathan (1862-1939), under-secretary for Ireland. The letter requests permission for army funds to be used in order to pay a secretary's wages.Sir Matthew Nathan was the under-secretary for Ireland from 1914 to 1916. In 1916 Nathan offered his resignation following the outbreak of the Easter Rising.

msIdentifier.collection: Chief Secretary’s Office Registered papers, CSO RP/1916/8860/1

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Any further communication on this
subject should be addressed to â
The Secretary,
War Office,
London, S.W.,
and the following number quoted.
B.M. 5920 (F.2) War Office, London, S.W. 23rd May 1916. Sir, I am commanded by the Army Council to acquaint
you that, in connection with the loan of the services of
Mr. G.P. Kurten of your Department to the Department of
Recruiting for Ireland under the Lord Lieutenant, it has been
represented that this gentleman was in receipt of an
allowance of Â£100 a year as private secretary to the Under
Secretary, and that his services were lent to the Department
for two months, his allowance being paid by the Under
Secretary. Mr Kurten was then seconded by the Under
Secretary for one month (January 1916), and for that month
the Lord Lieutenant agreed that the allowance at the rate of
Â£100 per annum should be borne by the Department of Recruiting
for Ireland. As the expenses of the Department, including the
above allowance, are being charged against Army funds, the
Council would be glad to be informed, before proceeding to
obtain Treasury authority for payment of the allowance, of the
exact status and pay of this gentleman, and whether the Chief
Secretary concurs in the arrangment. I am,
Sir,
Your obedient Servant, BB Cubitt The Under Secretary, Chief Secretary's Office, Dublin Castle. Mr. Holland
On what date
did Mr. Kurten
cease to draw the
allowance of Â£100
as P. Secy.?
J.L.T.
25. V. 16.
Mr. Taylor,
31 Dec. 1915.
P.u.d.
25/5/16
